questions.question: What are the two main types of virtualization and how do they differ?
keyPoints: Full Virtualization: Presents a complete simulation of the underlying hardware, allowing an unmodified guest OS to run. The hypervisor (VMM) handles all privileged instructions.
Para-virtualization: Requires a modified guest OS that is aware it is being virtualized. The guest OS communicates directly with the hypervisor for performance gains, rather than emulating hardware.
Key Difference: Full virtualization runs an OS unchanged, while para-virtualization requires OS modifications for more efficient operation.

questions.question: Explain the primary characteristic that distinguishes a distributed operating system from a network operating system.
keyPoints: A distributed OS presents a single, unified system image to its users, making a collection of independent computers appear as a single coherent system.
In contrast, a network OS runs on independent computers that coordinate and communicate with each other but maintain their own individual user environments and resource management.
The key distinction is transparency; a distributed OS hides the autonomous nature of the nodes, while a network OS makes it apparent.
